name: Taj Mahal
heroCaption: Taj Mahal at sunrise, Agra
fieldNotes: An ivory-white marble mausoleum on the south bank of the Yamuna, built by Mughal Emperor Shah Jahan in memory of his wife Mumtaz Mahal. A UNESCO World Heritage Site and one of the New Seven Wonders of the World.

entryFees.notes: Closed on Fridays. Moonlight viewing available on full moon nights.

stayOptions: Wide range from budget guesthouses in Taj Ganj to luxury hotels like Oberoi Amarvilas with direct Taj views.
permits: No special permits. Online tickets at asi.payumoney.com recommended to avoid queues.

name: Varanasi Ghats
heroCaption: Evening Ganga Aarti at Dashashwamedh Ghat
fieldNotes: One of the world's oldest living cities. The sacred ghats along the Ganges are a living tapestry of life, death, and spirituality — where pilgrims bathe, priests chant, and the evening Ganga Aarti sets the river ablaze with lamps.

entryFees.notes: Boat rides along the ghats: ₹100–500 per person.

stayOptions: Guesthouses along the ghats for an authentic experience. Luxury options available at BrijRama Palace.
permits: No permits required. Respectful dress code expected near temples.

name: Golden Temple
heroCaption: Harmandir Sahib gleaming at night, Amritsar
fieldNotes: The holiest shrine in Sikhism, Sri Harmandir Sahib (The Abode of God) is a breathtaking structure of gold-plated marble surrounded by the Amrit Sarovar (Pool of Nectar). Open to everyone regardless of faith.

entryFees.notes: Head covering and washed feet required before entry.

stayOptions: Free Saraie (pilgrim accommodation) available. Luxury hotels nearby in Amritsar.
permits: No permits required. No leather items allowed inside.

name: Hampi Ruins
heroCaption: Ancient Vijayanagara ruins among boulders, Hampi
fieldNotes: A UNESCO World Heritage Site and the former capital of the great Vijayanagara Empire. Bizarre boulder-strewn landscapes frame over 1,600 surviving remains of temples, palaces, and bazaars dating back to the 14th century.

entryFees.notes: Virupaksha Temple free to enter. Archaeological site ticket separate.

stayOptions: Guesthouses in Hampi Bazaar and Virupapur Gaddi. Limited options — book ahead in peak season.
permits: No special permits. ASI ticket required for main enclosure.

name: Amber Fort
heroCaption: Amber Fort overlooking Maota Lake, Jaipur
fieldNotes: Perched on a rocky hillside and overlooking Maota Lake, Amber Fort is the crown jewel of Jaipur. Built in 1592 by Raja Man Singh, its blend of Rajput and Mughal architecture is a visual masterpiece — intricate mirror work, ornate gates, and sprawling courtyards.

entryFees.notes: Light & Sound show tickets separate: ₹200–295.

stayOptions: Stay in Jaipur city — wide range from heritage havelis to luxury resorts.
permits: No special permits. Photography inside requires ₹100 camera fee.

name: Jaisalmer Fort
heroCaption: The golden Sonar Quila rising from the Thar Desert
fieldNotes: Rising dramatically from the golden sands of the Thar Desert, Jaisalmer Fort (Sonar Quila) is one of the world's few living forts — a quarter of the city's population still resides within its ancient walls. A UNESCO World Heritage Site.

entryFees.notes: Desert safaris from Jaisalmer: ₹1,000–3,000 per person.

stayOptions: Camps in the Thar Desert dunes, or heritage havelis inside the fort walls.
permits: No special permits. Entry to fort palaces requires ticket.

name: Mehrangarh Fort
heroCaption: Mehrangarh towering above the Blue City of Jodhpur
fieldNotes: One of India's largest forts, Mehrangarh rises 125 metres above the 'Blue City' of Jodhpur. Its massive walls, intricate carvings, and sweeping views of the blue-painted old city make it one of the most spectacular fortresses in all of India.

entryFees.notes: Audio guide available. Zip-line experience extra.

stayOptions: Stay in Jodhpur old city for authentic experience. RAAS Jodhpur is a renowned boutique heritage hotel.
permits: No special permits required.

name: Mysore Palace
heroCaption: Mysore Palace illuminated at night
fieldNotes: The official residence of the Wadiyar dynasty and seat of the Kingdom of Mysore, this Indo-Saracenic masterpiece is among the most visited monuments in India. It is famously illuminated by 97,000 light bulbs on Sundays and during Dasara festival.

entryFees.notes: No photography inside the palace. Phone must be deposited.

stayOptions: Mysore is well connected. Stay at heritage hotels like Lalitha Mahal Palace Hotel.
permits: No special permits. Bags must be checked at entry.

name: Ajanta & Ellora Caves
heroCaption: Rock-cut Buddhist cave murals at Ajanta
fieldNotes: Two extraordinary UNESCO World Heritage Sites in Maharashtra. Ajanta's 30 rock-cut Buddhist cave monasteries contain some of the finest surviving examples of ancient Indian murals. Ellora's 34 caves represent Buddhist, Hindu, and Jain traditions side by side.

entryFees.notes: Ajanta closed on Mondays. Ellora closed on Tuesdays.

stayOptions: Stay in Aurangabad (30 km from Ellora, 100 km from Ajanta). Luxury options at Vivanta Aurangabad.
permits: No special permits. Video cameras attract additional fee.

name: Khajuraho Temples
heroCaption: Intricate erotic carvings on Khajuraho temple facades
fieldNotes: A group of Hindu and Jain temples built between 950–1050 CE, celebrated for their exquisite nagara-style architecture and erotic sculptures. A UNESCO World Heritage Site that stands as a pinnacle of medieval Indian art.

entryFees.notes: Western group entry ticket covers main temples.

stayOptions: Small town with decent hotel options. Lalit Temple View is a good choice for views of the temples.
permits: No special permits.

name: Ranthambore National Park
heroCaption: Royal Bengal Tiger in Ranthambore
fieldNotes: One of India's best tiger reserves, Ranthambore is famed for its tigers being visible in daylight around ancient ruins and lakes — unlike most other parks. The historic Ranthambore Fort overlooks the tiger habitat, adding a dramatic backdrop.

entryFees.notes: Park closed July–September for monsoon. Advance booking essential.

stayOptions: Sawai Madhopur town has many safari lodges. Aman-i-Khás and KHEM Villas are top choices.
permits: Online booking mandatory via rajasthanwildlife.in. Slots fill up fast.

name: Kaziranga National Park
heroCaption: One-horned rhinoceros grazing in Kaziranga
fieldNotes: A UNESCO World Heritage Site holding two-thirds of the world's one-horned rhinoceros population. The park's dense tall elephant grass, semi-tropical forests, and interconnected streams also support the world's highest density of tigers.

entryFees.notes: Elephant safaris: ₹1,400 per person. Park closed May–October.

stayOptions: Forest lodges inside the park and resorts in Kohora village. Infinitea and Wild Grass Lodge are popular.
permits: Park entry permits required. Elephant safari permits often booked weeks in advance.

name: Valley of Flowers
heroCaption: Meadows of endemic flowers, Valley of Flowers
fieldNotes: A UNESCO World Heritage Site in the Western Himalayas, accessible only on foot. This national park is renowned for its meadows of endemic alpine flowers and diverse fauna. Open only July–September during blooming season — a truly other-worldly experience.

entryFees.notes: Only accessible July–September. 17 km trek from Govindghat.

stayOptions: Camping not allowed inside. Stay in Ghangaria village (3 km from entry). Basic guesthouses only.
permits: Forest Department permit required. Register at Govindghat before trekking.

name: Manali
heroCaption: Snow-capped peaks above the Beas River Valley, Manali
fieldNotes: Nestled in the mountains of the Beas River Valley at 2,050 m altitude, Manali is the adventure capital of Himachal Pradesh. Gateway to Lahaul-Spiti valley and Rohtang Pass, it offers everything from skiing and paragliding to quiet riverside walks.

entryFees.notes: Rohtang Pass requires permit ₹500 per vehicle. Book online.

stayOptions: Wide range from backpacker hostels in old Manali to luxury resorts. Solang Valley has ski resorts.
permits: Rohtang Pass permit mandatory (online booking only, limited daily slots).

name: Darjeeling
heroCaption: Kanchenjunga from Tiger Hill, Darjeeling at dawn
fieldNotes: The 'Queen of Hills', Darjeeling sits at 2,042 m with dramatic views of Kanchenjunga, the world's third highest peak. Famous for its tea gardens, the nostalgic Darjeeling Himalayan Railway (a UNESCO heritage site), and warm Nepali-Tibetan culture.

entryFees.notes: Toy train UNESCO joy ride: ₹240 (Indian), ₹1,000 (foreign).

stayOptions: Heritage hotels like Windamere Hotel and The Elgin. Tea estate stays available.
permits: No special permits. Inner Line Permit required for Sikkim onward.

name: Munnar
heroCaption: Rolling tea estates carpeting the hills of Munnar
fieldNotes: A stunning hill station in Kerala's Western Ghats, Munnar is famous for its endless emerald tea plantations rolling over misty mountain tops. Home to the Neelakurinji flower that blooms only once every 12 years, and Eravikulam National Park.

entryFees.notes: Eravikulam NP entry: ₹125 Indian, ₹400 foreign.

stayOptions: Tea estate bungalows for an immersive experience. Wide range in Munnar town.
permits: No special permits. Eravikulam NP may close during peak season.

name: Coorg
heroCaption: Coffee and spice plantations blanketing the Coorg hills
fieldNotes: Known as the 'Scotland of India', Coorg (Kodagu) is a misty highland region blanketed in coffee, cardamom, and pepper plantations. The Kodava people have a unique warrior culture, and the landscape is dotted with cascading waterfalls and monasteries.

entryFees.notes: Plantation stays often include guided coffee tours.

stayOptions: Plantation homestays are the authentic way to experience Coorg. Range from budget to luxury.
permits: No permits required.

name: Goa Beaches
heroCaption: Golden sands and palm trees on a North Goa beach
fieldNotes: India's smallest state packs in 105 km of coastline spanning from the party beaches of North Goa to the serene, reef-fringed shores of South Goa. Portuguese colonial heritage, vibrant nightlife, spiced seafood, and year-round sunshine make it iconic.

entryFees.notes: Water sport prices vary. Scuba diving packages from ₹3,000.

stayOptions: Wide range from beach shacks and hostels to 5-star beach resorts. Book early for Christmas/New Year.
permits: No permits required for beaches.

name: Radhanagar Beach
heroCaption: Crystal turquoise waters of Radhanagar Beach, Havelock Island
fieldNotes: Consistently ranked among Asia's best beaches, Radhanagar (Beach No. 7) on Havelock Island has powder-white sand backed by dense tropical forest, and crystal-clear turquoise water. Spectacular sunsets and excellent snorkelling in the shallows.

entryFees.notes: Scuba diving ₹3,500–4,500. Ferry from Port Blair to Havelock.

stayOptions: Havelock Island has eco-resorts and boutique hotels. Barefoot at Havelock is the most famous.
permits: Andaman Restricted Area Permit mandatory for all visitors. Issued on arrival at Port Blair airport.

name: Varkala Beach
heroCaption: Dramatic red laterite cliffs above Varkala Beach
fieldNotes: Unlike any other beach in Kerala, Varkala's golden beach is dramatically backed by towering red laterite cliffs lined with cafes and yoga studios. Also a pilgrimage site — the ancient Janardhana Swami temple overlooks the sea.

entryFees.notes: Beach is free. Cliff-top yoga classes and ayurvedic centres widely available.

stayOptions: Cliff-top guesthouses with sea views. Budget to mid-range options. Cave Beach Resort for secluded stays.
permits: No permits required.

name: Meenakshi Amman Temple
heroCaption: Towering gopurams of Meenakshi Amman Temple, Madurai
fieldNotes: A historic Hindu temple complex dedicated to Goddess Meenakshi, a form of Parvati, and her consort Shiva. Its 14 gateway towers (gopurams) encrusted with thousands of brightly painted sculptures are among the most breathtaking examples of Dravidian architecture.

entryFees.notes: Non-Hindus not allowed inside sanctum. Camera strictly prohibited.

stayOptions: Wide range in Madurai. Heritage hotels like Visalam and Gateway Hotel Pasumalai.
permits: No permits. Dress code: traditional Indian attire preferred.

name: Qutub Minar
heroCaption: The soaring minaret of Qutb ud-Din Aibak, Delhi
fieldNotes: A UNESCO World Heritage Site and the world's tallest brick minaret at 72.5 m, built in 1193 by Qutb ud-Din Aibak. The complex also houses the 7th century iron pillar that has refused to rust for over 1,600 years — a metallurgical mystery.

entryFees.notes: Metro accessible - Qutab Minar station on Yellow Line.

stayOptions: Stay in South Delhi or central Delhi. Wide range of hotels in the city.
permits: No special permits.

name: Red Fort
heroCaption: The iconic red sandstone walls of Lal Qila, Old Delhi
fieldNotes: A UNESCO World Heritage Site and the main residence of the Mughal emperors for nearly 200 years. The Red Fort's massive red sandstone walls and the Lahore Gate are iconic symbols of India — the site from which the Prime Minister addresses the nation every Independence Day.

entryFees.notes: Light & Sound shows: ₹80 (Hindi), ₹100 (English).

stayOptions: Old Delhi budget hotels. Luxury options in Connaught Place area.
permits: No special permits. Security check mandatory.

name: Fatehpur Sikri
heroCaption: Buland Darwaza, the great gateway of Fatehpur Sikri
fieldNotes: Built by Emperor Akbar and abandoned after just 14 years due to water shortage, this UNESCO ghost city is remarkably preserved. Its Buland Darwaza at 54m is among the world's highest gateways. The entire city is a masterpiece of Mughal urban planning.

entryFees.notes: 40 km from Agra. Can combine with Taj Mahal in same day.

stayOptions: Day trip from Agra is most common. Small guesthouses available in Fatehpur Sikri town.
permits: No special permits.

name: Brihadeeswarar Temple
heroCaption: The towering vimana of Brihadeeswarar Temple, Thanjavur
fieldNotes: Built by Raja Raja Chola I in 1010 CE and a UNESCO World Heritage Site, this 66-metre tall Dravidian temple is a zenith of Chola architecture. The massive Nandi bull sculpture and the shadow-less dome are marvels of ancient engineering.

entryFees.notes: Light & Sound show in the evenings.

stayOptions: Stay in Thanjavur town. Ideal base for exploring the Chola heartland.
permits: No permits required.

name: Sundarbans
heroCaption: Dense mangrove channels of the Sundarbans delta
fieldNotes: The world's largest mangrove forest and a UNESCO World Heritage Site, home to the Royal Bengal Tiger — the only tiger population in the world that has adapted to swimming through tidal waterways. Accessible only by boat through labyrinthine channels.

entryFees.notes: Only accessible via organized boat tours from Kolkata. 2-3 night tours recommended.

stayOptions: Eco-lodges inside the buffer zone. Sajnakhali and Gosaba are main bases.
permits: Forest Department permit mandatory. Organized tour operators handle permits.

name: Shimla
heroCaption: Colonial-era architecture along The Mall Road, Shimla
fieldNotes: The former summer capital of British India, perched at 2,200 m in the Himalayas. Shimla's Victorian architecture, the famous toy train on the UNESCO-listed Kalka-Shimla Railway, and its apple orchards make it the most iconic hill station in India.

entryFees.notes: Toy train Shimla-Kalka: ₹290 (Indian), ₹790 (foreign).

stayOptions: Heritage hotels on Ridge Road with colonial charm. Wide range from budget to luxury.
permits: No permits required.

name: Ooty
heroCaption: The Nilgiri Mountain Railway ascending through tea estates
fieldNotes: The 'Queen of Hill Stations' sits at 2,240 m in the Nilgiri Hills. Famous for its tea estates, rose garden, and the iconic blue Nilgiri Mountain Railway — a UNESCO Heritage Site that winds through tunnels and verdant forests aboard a century-old steam train.

entryFees.notes: Nilgiri Railway: ₹25–70 (Indian), ₹285 (foreign).

stayOptions: Heritage cottages and club hotels. Savoy Hotel is a classic colonial hotel.
permits: No permits required.

name: Jim Corbett National Park
heroCaption: Bengal Tiger in Jim Corbett, India's oldest national park
fieldNotes: India's oldest national park and the first to come under Project Tiger in 1973. Named after the legendary hunter-turned-conservationist Jim Corbett, this Terai grassland park in the Himalayan foothills has one of India's highest tiger densities.

entryFees.notes: Park closed mid-June to mid-November. Dhikala zone is best for wildlife.

stayOptions: Luxury jungle lodges outside park boundary. Forest Rest Houses inside (book via park authority).
permits: Online booking mandatory via corbettonline.uk.gov.in. Dhikala requires 45-day advance.

name: Golconda Fort
heroCaption: Golconda Fort's massive granite bastions, Hyderabad
fieldNotes: Once the capital of the Qutb Shahi dynasty and the Mughal province of Hyderabad, Golconda was one of the world's leading diamond trading centres — the Koh-i-Noor and Hope Diamond originated here. Its ingenious acoustic system can carry a whistle from the gate to the top.

entryFees.notes: Light & Sound show evenings: ₹130–160.

stayOptions: Stay in Hyderabad city. Wide range from budget hotels in Abids to luxury in Banjara Hills.
permits: No special permits.
